---
title: "Glossary"
description: "Plain-language definitions for every term introduced across Modules 00–12."
---

Alphabetically ordered definitions for every bolded term introduced in the course.
Each entry gives a plain-language definition first, followed by technical precision.

---

## A

**Annotated tag**
A tag that stores a message, author, and date alongside the commit hash it points to.
Unlike a lightweight tag (which is just a hash pointer), an annotated tag is a full
Git object. Created with `git tag -a`. Preferred for release marking.
→ [Module 07](../modules/07-advanced-operations.qmd)

**Argument**
A value passed to a command that specifies what the command operates on (e.g., the
filename in `git add scripts/analysis.R`). Distinct from a flag/option, which modifies
behavior rather than specifying a target.
→ [Module 00](../modules/00-terminal-fundamentals.qmd)

---

## B

**Blob object**
A Git object that stores the raw content of a file, without the filename or path.
Two files with identical content share a single blob object. Git identifies blobs
by a SHA-1 hash of their content. Viewable with `git cat-file -p HASH`.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Branch**
A lightweight, movable pointer to a specific commit, stored as a text file in
`.git/refs/heads/`. Creating a branch is instantaneous — Git only writes a 41-byte file.
When you commit, Git rewrites the branch file with the new commit's hash.
→ [Module 04](../modules/04-branching.qmd)

---

## C

**Cherry-pick**
The act of copying a specific commit from one branch to another without merging
the entire branch. Creates a new commit with the same changes but a different hash.
Useful for applying a targeted fix across branches. Command: `git cherry-pick HASH`.
→ [Module 07](../modules/07-advanced-operations.qmd)

**Commit**
A snapshot of all tracked files at a specific moment, stored permanently in
`.git/objects/`. Each commit contains: a pointer to a tree object, the author's name
and email, a timestamp, the commit message, and a pointer to the parent commit(s).
Commits form a directed acyclic graph (DAG).
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Commit graph**
See **DAG**.

**Commit message**
A human-readable description attached to a commit. Research-grade messages follow
the format `Type: description` (e.g., `Fix: use z-score normalization per Smith 2024`).
The message is stored inside the commit object and is permanent.
→ [Module 02](../modules/02-basic-workflow.qmd)

**Commit object**
The internal Git representation of a commit, stored as a compressed file in
`.git/objects/`. Contains: tree hash, parent hash(es), author, committer, timestamp,
and message. Viewable with `git cat-file -p COMMIT_HASH`.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Conflict marker**
One of three special lines Git inserts into a file during a merge or rebase conflict:
`<<<<<<< HEAD` (start of current branch version), `=======` (divider), and
`>>>>>>> branch-name` (end of incoming version). Must be removed by the developer
before staging the resolved file.
→ [Module 05](../modules/05-merging.qmd)

---

## D

**DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**
The mathematical structure Git uses to represent commit history. Each commit points
to its parent(s) — the graph is *directed* (arrows go from child to parent) and
*acyclic* (no cycles — a commit cannot be its own ancestor). Branches are pointers
into this graph.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Dangling object**
A Git object (commit, tree, or blob) that is not reachable from any branch, tag,
or other reference. Created by operations like `git reset --hard` or abandoned
rebase attempts. Dangling objects are recoverable via `git reflog` until garbage
collection runs (approximately every 30 days).
→ [Module 10](../modules/10-troubleshooting.qmd)

**Default branch**
The primary branch in a repository, typically named `main`. Set with
`git config --global init.defaultBranch main`. GitHub's default for new repositories
is `main` (changed from `master` in 2020).
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Detached HEAD**
A state where `HEAD` points directly to a commit hash rather than a branch name.
Occurs when checking out a specific commit, tag, or PR. Commits made in detached
HEAD are not on any branch and can be lost when switching away. Escape by creating
a branch: `git switch -c rescue-branch`.
→ [Module 10](../modules/10-troubleshooting.qmd)

**diff**
The representation of changes between two versions of a file or tree. Lines prefixed
with `+` were added; lines prefixed with `-` were removed. Git generates diffs using
the Myers diff algorithm. Viewable with `git diff`, `git diff --staged`, or `git show`.
→ [Module 02](../modules/02-basic-workflow.qmd)

---

## F

**Fast-forward merge**
A merge strategy where the target branch (e.g., `main`) simply moves its pointer
forward to match the incoming branch, without creating a merge commit. Possible only
when the incoming branch is directly ahead of the target with no divergence.
→ [Module 05](../modules/05-merging.qmd)

**Flag / Option**
A modifier to a command that changes its behavior, typically prefixed with `-` (short
form) or `--` (long form). Example: `git log --oneline` uses the `--oneline` flag.
→ [Module 00](../modules/00-terminal-fundamentals.qmd)

**Fork**
A personal copy of another repository on GitHub, owned by your account. A fork gives
you write access to your copy while leaving the original unchanged. Used in the
forking workflow: fork → clone → branch → PR → upstream sync.
→ [Module 09](../modules/09-collaboration.qmd)

---

## G

**Garbage collection**
Git's automatic process (triggered by `git gc`) that compresses loose objects into
pack files, removes unreachable dangling objects older than the grace period
(default: 30 days), and reclaims disk space.
→ [Module 08](../modules/08-data-management.qmd)

**git**
A distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds in 2005.
"Distributed" means every clone is a complete repository with full history — there
is no single server that is authoritative (GitHub is a convention, not a requirement).
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**gitignore**
A file named `.gitignore` containing patterns for files and directories that Git
should not track. Patterns use glob syntax (`*`, `?`, `**`). Only applies to files
not yet tracked — already-committed files require `git rm --cached` to stop tracking.
→ [Module 08](../modules/08-data-management.qmd)

**Git LFS (Large File Storage)**
A Git extension that replaces large file content with a pointer in the Git object
database, storing the actual file content on a separate LFS server. Required for
files too large for standard Git storage (neuroimaging, video, large tabular data).
→ [Module 08](../modules/08-data-management.qmd)

---

## H

**Hash (SHA-1)**
A 40-character hexadecimal string that uniquely identifies a Git object.
Computed as a cryptographic hash of the object's content — identical content always
produces the same hash. Git uses hashes instead of filenames to store objects.
Example: `3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2d`.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**HEAD**
A special pointer that indicates which branch (or commit) is currently checked out.
Stored in `.git/HEAD` as either a branch reference (`ref: refs/heads/main`) or a
raw commit hash (when in detached HEAD state). HEAD is updated by `git switch`,
`git commit`, `git reset`, and `git checkout`.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Hook**
A shell script in `.git/hooks/` that Git runs automatically at specific workflow
points. A `pre-commit` hook runs before every commit; a `post-merge` hook runs
after every merge. Hooks are local (not committed) and must be made executable
with `chmod +x`.
→ [Module 11](../modules/11-tool-integration.qmd)

---

## I

**Index**
See **Staging area**.

**Interactive rebase**
A rebase mode (`git rebase -i`) that opens a text editor showing each commit as a
line in a "todo list". The developer can reorder, squash, fixup, reword, edit, or
drop commits before they are replayed. Used to clean up local history before sharing.
→ [Module 06](../modules/06-rebase.qmd)

---

## M

**Merge**
The act of combining the histories of two branches. Git finds the common ancestor,
computes the changes in each branch, and combines them. Results in either a
fast-forward (no new commit) or a three-way merge commit.
→ [Module 05](../modules/05-merging.qmd)

**Merge commit**
A commit with two parent commits, created when two diverged branches are merged.
Visually appears as the "diamond" in `git log --graph`. Contains the combined state
of both branches at the point of merging.
→ [Module 05](../modules/05-merging.qmd)

---

## O

**Object**
Git's fundamental storage unit. Every file version, directory listing, commit, and
tag is stored as an object in `.git/objects/`. The four types are: blob (file content),
tree (directory listing), commit, and tag. Objects are content-addressed by hash.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Origin**
The conventional name for the primary remote repository. Set automatically when you
`git clone` or added manually with `git remote add origin URL`. `origin/main` is the
remote-tracking branch for the `main` branch on the remote named `origin`.
→ [Module 03](../modules/03-remotes.qmd)

---

## P

**Pack file**
A compressed binary file in `.git/objects/pack/` containing many Git objects stored
together. Pack files are more space-efficient than loose objects and are created
by `git gc` or during `git push`/`git clone`.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**Pickaxe search**
Informal name for `git log -S "string"` — searches for commits where the count of
a specific string changed (i.e., the string was added or removed). Named after the
`-S` flag's original code reference.
→ [Module 07](../modules/07-advanced-operations.qmd)

**Pull request (PR)**
A request to merge changes from one branch (or fork) into another, typically reviewed
by at least one other person before merging. Despite the name, a pull request is a
GitHub/GitLab concept, not a core Git feature. Created from the terminal with `gh pr create`.
→ [Module 09](../modules/09-collaboration.qmd)

---

## R

**Rebase**
A Git operation that replays commits from one branch onto a different base commit.
Changes the parent of each replayed commit, resulting in new hashes. Creates linear
history without merge commits. Never rebase shared/published history.
→ [Module 06](../modules/06-rebase.qmd)

**Reflog**
A local log of all HEAD movements, stored in `.git/logs/HEAD`. Records every commit,
reset, checkout, merge, and rebase. Used to recover commits after `git reset --hard`
or accidental branch deletion. Entries expire after approximately 30 days.
→ [Module 10](../modules/10-troubleshooting.qmd)

**Remote**
A named URL pointing to another copy of the repository, typically on GitHub.
Managed with `git remote`. The conventional name for the primary remote is `origin`;
`upstream` is used for the original repository when working with forks.
→ [Module 03](../modules/03-remotes.qmd)

**Repository (repo)**
A directory tracked by Git, containing a `.git/` subdirectory that stores all
version history, configuration, and objects. Can be local (on your machine) or
remote (on GitHub). A clone is a complete, independent copy of a repository.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

---

## S

**Semantic versioning**
A version numbering convention using MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., v1.2.3):
MAJOR increments for breaking changes, MINOR for new features, PATCH for bug fixes.
Applied to analysis code via annotated Git tags to mark manuscript submission points.
→ [Module 12](../modules/12-professional-repos.qmd)

**Snapshot**
Git's model for storing file versions — each commit records the complete state of
all tracked files at that moment (not just the changes). Internally Git avoids
storing duplicate content by reusing blob objects with identical hashes.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

**SSH key**
An asymmetric cryptographic key pair used to authenticate with remote Git hosts
without a password. The private key (`~/.ssh/id_ed25519`) stays on your machine;
the public key (`~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`) is uploaded to GitHub. The Ed25519 algorithm
is recommended for new keys.
→ [Module 03](../modules/03-remotes.qmd)

**Squash**
Combining multiple commits into one. In interactive rebase, `squash` folds a commit
into the previous one and opens an editor for the combined message; `fixup` does the
same but discards the later commit's message silently.
→ [Module 06](../modules/06-rebase.qmd)

**Staging area (index)**
An intermediate area between the working directory and the repository where changes
are assembled before committing. Files are added to the staging area with `git add`
and removed with `git restore --staged`. The staging area allows composing commits
from a subset of working directory changes.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd), [Module 02](../modules/02-basic-workflow.qmd)

**Stash**
A temporary storage area for uncommitted working directory changes. Created with
`git stash save`. Changes are saved to a stack (`git stash list`), can be restored
with `git stash pop`, and are lost if the repository is deleted. Local only — stashes
do not push to remotes.
→ [Module 07](../modules/07-advanced-operations.qmd)

---

## T

**Terminal**
A text-based interface for interacting with a computer's operating system via typed
commands. On macOS: Terminal.app or iTerm2. On Linux: the built-in terminal emulator.
On Windows: Git Bash or Windows Terminal with WSL2.
→ [Module 00](../modules/00-terminal-fundamentals.qmd)

**Three-way merge**
A merge strategy used when both branches have diverged from their common ancestor.
Git computes the diff from the ancestor to each branch tip and combines them.
If both branches modified the same lines, a conflict results. Creates a merge commit.
→ [Module 05](../modules/05-merging.qmd)

**Tracked file**
A file that Git is monitoring for changes — it was added with `git add` at least once
and is included in the most recent commit. Opposite of an untracked file (new, never added)
or an ignored file (matches a `.gitignore` pattern).
→ [Module 02](../modules/02-basic-workflow.qmd)

**Tree object**
A Git object that represents a directory listing, mapping filenames and permissions
to blob (file) or tree (subdirectory) hashes. Every commit points to one root tree
object that describes the complete file system state at that snapshot.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)

---

## U

**Upstream**
1. A remote named `upstream` — conventionally the original repository you forked from,
   as opposed to your personal fork (`origin`).
2. A tracking relationship: a local branch that is configured to track a remote branch
   is said to have an upstream. Set with `git push -u origin BRANCH`.
→ [Module 09](../modules/09-collaboration.qmd)

---

## W

**Working directory (working tree)**
The directory on your local filesystem where files appear in their current, editable
state. Changes made here are not saved to Git until staged and committed. Three states
apply to files: untracked, modified (tracked but changed), or staged.
→ [Module 01](../modules/01-git-concepts.qmd)
